examples.title: Simple CNN Implementation
examples.description: Basic CNN architecture for image classification using PyTorch
examples.code:
import torch.nn as nn

class SimpleCNN(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes=10):
        super(SimpleCNN, self).__init__()
        # Conv Layer 1: 3 input channels, 32 filters, 3x3 kernel
        self.conv1 = nn.Conv2d(3, 32, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
        self.bn1 = nn.BatchNorm2d(32)
        self.relu1 = nn.ReLU()
        self.pool1 = nn.MaxPool2d(kernel_size=2, stride=2)
        
        # Conv Layer 2: 32 input channels, 64 filters
        self.conv2 = nn.Conv2d(32, 64, kernel_size=3, padding=1)
        self.bn2 = nn.BatchNorm2d(64)
        self.relu2 = nn.ReLU()
        self.pool2 = nn.MaxPool2d(kernel_size=2, stride=2)
        
        # Fully Connected Layers
        self.fc1 = nn.Linear(64 * 8 * 8, 256)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(0.5)
        self.fc2 = nn.Linear(256, num_classes)
    
    def forward(self, x):
        # Conv block 1: 32x32 -> 16x16
        x = self.pool1(self.relu1(self.bn1(self.conv1(x))))
        # Conv block 2: 16x16 -> 8x8
        x = self.pool2(self.relu2(self.bn2(self.conv2(x))))
        # Flatten and fully connected
        x = x.view(x.size(0), -1)
        x = self.dropout(self.fc1(x))
        x = self.fc2(x)
        return x
examples.explanation: This CNN has 2 convolutional blocks with batch normalization, ReLU activation, and max pooling, followed by fully connected layers with dropout for classification.

examples.title: Convolution Forward Pass
examples.description: Pseudocode for the convolution operation in CNNs
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the forward pass of convolution: sliding each kernel across the padded input, computing element-wise multiplication and summation to produce feature maps.

examples.title: Residual Block Implementation
examples.description: Java implementation of a ResNet residual block
examples.explanation: This Java class implements a ResNet residual block with skip connection. The forward pass computes F(x) through two conv layers, then adds the identity input x, enabling gradient flow for very deep networks.

examples.title: VGG Block and VGG Network
examples.description: Python implementation of VGG-style network showing the 3x3 conv stack pattern
examples.code:
def vgg_block(num_convs, in_channels, out_channels):
    """VGG block with n stacked 3x3 convolutions"""
    layers = []
    for _ in range(num_convs):
        layers.append(nn.Conv2d(in_channels, out_channels, 
                                 kernel_size=3, padding=1))
        layers.append(nn.BatchNorm2d(out_channels))
        layers.append(nn.ReLU(inplace=True))
        in_channels = out_channels
    layers.append(nn.MaxPool2d(kernel_size=2, stride=2))
    return nn.Sequential(*layers)

class VGGNet(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_classes=1000):
        super(VGGNet, self).__init__()
        # VGG-16 configuration
        self.features = nn.Sequential(
            # Block 1: 224x224 -> 112x112
            vgg_block(2, 3, 64),
            # Block 2: 112x112 -> 56x56
            vgg_block(2, 64, 128),
            # Block 3: 56x56 -> 28x28
            vgg_block(3, 128, 256),
            # Block 4: 28x28 -> 14x14
            vgg_block(3, 256, 512),
            # Block 5: 14x14 -> 7x7
            vgg_block(3, 512, 512),
        )
        
        # Global Average Pooling
        self.avgpool = nn.AdaptiveAvgPool2d((7, 7))
        
        # Fully connected layers
        self.classifier = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(512 * 7 * 7, 4096),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(0.5),
            nn.Linear(4096, 4096),
            nn.ReLU(inplace=True),
            nn.Dropout(0.5),
            nn.Linear(4096, num_classes),
        )
    
    def forward(self, x):
        x = self.features(x)
        x = self.avgpool(x)
        x = torch.flatten(x, 1)
        x = self.classifier(x)
        return x
examples.explanation: This VGG-16 implementation shows the key insight: using only 3x3 convolutions stacked (vs larger filters) achieves larger receptive fields with fewer parameters. Five blocks create 16 weight layers.
